transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Sydney. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my stomach issues lately. It’s just been really stressful, and I can’t help but worry about whether things will ever get better. I mean, I try to follow the advice I get, but sometimes it feels like nothing is really working. It’s just... hard to feel in control, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your stomach issues are causing you a lot of stress and worry, especially when it feels like you're not seeing the results you hope for. It’s understandable to feel a lack of control in that situation. Can you tell me more about what specific aspects of this experience feel the hardest for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... I keep thinking about all the things I might be doing wrong. Like, am I eating the right foods? Should I be doing something different? I find myself avoiding certain things just in case they make it worse, but then I end up feeling anxious about what I can eat. It’s like I’m constantly second-guessing myself. I just wish I could feel more certain about what’s going on with my body.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a lot of pressure to make the right choices about your diet, and that uncertainty is adding to your anxiety. It must be really frustrating to feel like you're constantly second-guessing yourself. When you think about the foods you avoid, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I feel... worried, mostly. Like, what if I eat something and it makes things worse? It’s hard to shake off that fear. Sometimes I even feel a bit defeated, like no matter what I do, I can’t seem to get it right. It’s just this cycle of trying to be careful but then feeling anxious about it all. I don’t know, it just feels overwhelming at times.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really tough, Sydney. It seems like the worry and fear of making things worse are creating a cycle that feels overwhelming. Feeling defeated when you’re trying to be careful can be exhausting. When you think about that sense of defeat, how does it affect your day-to-day life or your mood?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It definitely makes things harder. Some days, I just feel really low and unmotivated. Like, I don’t want to do much because I’m so preoccupied with my stomach and what I should or shouldn’t be eating. It affects my mood, and I find myself getting irritable or just... distant from people. I know I should probably talk to someone about it, but it’s hard to open up. I don’t want to burden anyone with my worries.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your stomach issues are not only affecting your physical well-being but also your emotional state and relationships. Feeling low and irritable can make it tough to connect with others, and I can see why you might hesitate to share your worries. It’s understandable to feel that way, but it’s also important to have support. What do you think holds you back from opening up to others about what you’re experiencing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think part of it is just feeling like I don’t want to be a burden. I mean, everyone has their own stuff going on, right? I don’t want to add to that. Plus, sometimes I feel like people might not really understand what I’m going through. It’s hard to explain how much it weighs on me. So, I guess I just keep it to myself, even though it can feel really isolating.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes sense that you would feel hesitant to share your struggles, especially if you’re worried about burdening others or feeling misunderstood. That sense of isolation can be really heavy, especially when you’re dealing with something that weighs on you so much. I wonder, have there been times when you did share your feelings with someone, and how did that go for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, there have been a couple of times when I tried to talk about it, but it didn’t always go as I hoped. Sometimes, I felt like the person didn’t really get it or they brushed it off, which made me feel even more alone. Other times, they were supportive, but I still felt like I was holding back a lot. It’s just... hard to find the right words, and I worry about how they’ll react. So, I guess I just end up keeping a lot of it inside.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you've had mixed experiences when trying to share your feelings, which can make it even more challenging to open up. Feeling brushed off or misunderstood can deepen that sense of loneliness, and it’s understandable that you would hold back when it’s hard to find the right words. It’s a tough balance to navigate. When you think about the times you felt supported, what do you think made those moments different?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hmm, I think when I felt supported, it was usually because the person really listened and didn’t try to fix things right away. They just let me express what I was feeling without judgment. It felt nice to have someone acknowledge that it was tough for me. But even then, I still felt like I was holding back some of the deeper worries. I guess it’s just hard to fully let go of that fear of being vulnerable.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having someone who listens without trying to fix things makes a big difference for you. That kind of validation can be really comforting. It’s completely understandable to feel hesitant about being vulnerable, especially when it comes to deeper worries. Vulnerability can feel risky. What do you think would help you feel more comfortable expressing those deeper feelings when you’re ready?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe having a safe space where I know it’s okay to share without judgment would help. Like, if I felt that the person really wanted to understand and wasn’t going to rush me or dismiss my feelings, I might feel more comfortable. It’s just... I guess I need to build that trust first. And maybe taking small steps to share a little bit at a time could help, too. It feels less overwhelming that way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That makes a lot of sense. Having a safe space and building trust gradually can create a more comfortable environment for you to share your feelings. Taking small steps sounds like a thoughtful approach, allowing you to open up at your own pace. It’s important to feel secure in those conversations. As you think about building that trust, are there specific qualities or actions in others that help you feel more at ease?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think qualities like patience and empathy really help. When someone takes the time to really listen and shows that they care, it makes a big difference. I also appreciate when people ask open-ended questions instead of jumping to conclusions or solutions. It feels more like a conversation rather than an interrogation. Just knowing that someone is there to support me without pressure would really help me feel more at ease.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like patience and empathy are key for you in feeling supported, and that open-ended questions can create a more genuine dialogue. It’s great that you have a clear sense of what helps you feel more at ease. Knowing that someone is there to support you without pressure can be really comforting. As you think about your own needs in these conversations, how do you feel about expressing those needs to others when the time comes?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s a bit daunting, to be honest. I want to be clear about what I need, but I worry about how it’ll come across. What if they don’t understand or think I’m asking too much? I guess I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to express my needs, especially if it helps me feel more comfortable. It’s just... a bit scary to put myself out there like that. But I know it could really make a difference in how I connect with others.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel daunted by the idea of expressing your needs, especially when you’re concerned about how it might be received. It’s a vulnerable thing to do, but it sounds like you recognize the potential benefits it could have for your connections with others. Reminding yourself that it’s okay to express your needs is an important step. What do you think might help you feel more confident in expressing those needs when the opportunity arises?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe practicing what I want to say could help. Like, if I have a clearer idea of how to express my needs, it might feel less intimidating. Also, reminding myself that it’s okay to ask for support could make a difference. Maybe even talking about it with someone I trust beforehand could help me feel more prepared. Just having a little more clarity and confidence in what I want to say might make it easier when the time comes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan. Practicing what you want to say and having a clearer idea can definitely help reduce that intimidation factor. It’s also great that you’re considering talking it over with someone you trust beforehand—that can provide additional support and reassurance. Building that clarity and confidence is a valuable step toward feeling more comfortable expressing your needs. As you think about these strategies, how do you feel about taking those first steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of nervousness and hope, I guess. The idea of actually taking those first steps is a bit scary, but I also think it could be really beneficial. It’s just about finding the right moment and feeling ready. I know it won’t happen overnight, but even just thinking about it feels like a step in the right direction. I want to try, but I also know I’ll need to be patient with myself as I work through it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely natural to feel both nervousness and hope as you consider taking those first steps. Acknowledging that it’s a process and that it won’t happen overnight shows a lot of self-awareness and compassion for yourself. It’s encouraging to hear that you want to try and are willing to be patient with yourself along the way. What do you think might be the first small step you’d like to take as you begin this journey?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe the first small step could be to write down some of my thoughts and feelings about what I need from others. Just getting it all out on paper might help me clarify things. Then, I could practice saying it out loud, even if it’s just to myself. That way, when an opportunity comes up, I’ll feel a bit more prepared. It feels manageable, and I think it could help me build that confidence to express myself when the time is right.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful first step, Sydney. Writing down your thoughts and feelings can be a powerful way to clarify what you need, and practicing saying it out loud can help you feel more comfortable when the time comes. It’s great that you’re approaching this in a manageable way that feels right for you. As you start this process, how do you think you’ll feel once you’ve taken that first step?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ll feel a sense of relief, honestly. Just getting my thoughts down and practicing might help ease some of the anxiety I have about expressing myself. It could be empowering to take that first step, even if it’s small. I might feel a little more in control, knowing that I’m actively working on something that’s been weighing on me. I hope it’ll give me a bit of confidence to keep moving forward, too.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sense of relief and empowerment sounds really promising. Taking that first step can indeed help you feel more in control and may boost your confidence as you continue to navigate your feelings and needs. It’s wonderful that you’re recognizing the potential for positive change. As you move forward with this, remember to be gentle with yourself and celebrate those small victories along the way. Is there anything else on your mind today that you’d like to explore or discuss?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I appreciate that. I think I’ve shared a lot about my feelings and thoughts today, and it’s been helpful to talk through everything. I guess I just want to make sure I keep this momentum going and don’t fall back into old patterns. Maybe we can talk more about strategies for staying on track next time? I want to make sure I’m taking care of myself as I work through this.
